title: Conduction Heat Transfer
summary: Conduction is heat diffusing through solids because temperature is not uniform. Most exam problems reduce to a thermal resistance network: temperature difference divided by resistance gives heat rate. Plane walls, cylinders, contact resistance, generation, fins, and lumped transients are variations on that same idea.
objectives: Recognize conduction as heat transfer through a solid or stationary medium due to a temperature gradient.
Use Fourier law and the thermal-resistance analogy for plane walls and cylindrical pipes.
Combine conduction, convection, and contact resistances in series/parallel without losing units.
Compute overall $U$ and heat rate for composite walls and pipes.
Handle internal heat generation, critical insulation radius, fins, and lumped capacitance transients.
Know when lumped capacitance is allowed by checking Biot number.
Translate a wall/pipe drawing into a clean resistance circuit.

blocks.text: What conduction is

blocks.title: Plain-English map
blocks.text: Conduction problems are usually less about calculus and more about drawing the heat path. Heat goes from hot to cold. Every layer resists that flow. Put the resistances in the same order the heat sees them, then use the same logic as an electric circuit: driving difference over resistance.

steps.label: Draw the heat path
steps.text: Plane wall, cylinder, composite wall, pipe, fin, or transient lump. Geometry decides the resistance formula.

steps.label: Write every resistance
steps.text: Conduction through material, convection at surfaces, contact resistance between layers. Keep per-area vs total resistance straight.

steps.label: Combine the network
steps.text: Series resistances add. Parallel paths split heat. The total heat rate is usually $\Delta T/R_{tot}$.

steps.label: Add special physics only if present
steps.text: Generation creates a different temperature profile. Fins add area but need efficiency/effectiveness. Lumped transient needs $Bi<0.1$.

steps.label: Check units and direction
steps.text: Heat rate in W, heat flux in W/m², thermal conductivity in W/(m K). Negative signs mainly tell direction.

blocks.text:
**Conduction** is heat transfer through a medium (solid or fluid at rest) driven by a temperature gradient, with **no macroscopic motion of matter**. Energy passes from more energetic particles to less energetic ones, always from hot to cold.

This chapter answers one practical question: given temperatures and geometry, **how much heat flows and what are the temperatures inside?** The master tool is an analogy with electrical circuits — temperature plays the role of voltage, heat rate the role of current, and each layer is a **thermal resistance**.

**Heat flux** $q''$ is power per unit area, units $\mathrm{W/m^2}$. **Heat rate** $q$ is total power, units $\mathrm{W}$, with $q = q''\,A$. Watch units: tutorials sometimes report $q/A$ (flux) and sometimes $q/L$ (per metre of pipe).

blocks.caption: Fourier's law, 1-D. $k$ = thermal conductivity ($\mathrm{W/m\cdot K}$). The minus sign means heat flows DOWN the gradient (toward lower $T$).

blocks.title: Read Fourier's law correctly
blocks.text: The heat rate is **proportional to** the area $A$, to $k$, and to the temperature gradient. It is NOT inversely proportional to $k$ or to the gradient. A bigger gradient or higher conductivity means MORE heat.
Plane wall (no generation)

For steady state, 1-D, no internal generation, the heat equation reduces to $\dfrac{d^2T}{dx^2}=0$, so the temperature profile is a **straight line**. Integrating Fourier's law across a wall of thickness $L$ and faces $T_{s1}, T_{s2}$ gives a constant heat rate.

blocks.caption: Plane-wall conduction. The heat rate is constant and independent of $x$; only the temperature drops linearly.

blocks.text: Heat rate $q$ behaves like electric current $I$, the temperature difference $\Delta T$ like voltage $\Delta V$, and each layer like a resistance $R$. Then $q = \Delta T / R$, exactly like Ohm's law $I=\Delta V/R_e$. Resistances in series add; the same $q$ flows through all of them.

blocks.caption: The three resistances you will use constantly. Convection resistance comes from Newton's law $q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$.

blocks.title: The analogy has conditions
blocks.text: The thermal-resistance / electrical analogy is valid **only at steady state and only WITHOUT volumetric generation**. The moment heat is generated inside the body, you cannot draw a simple resistance circuit — you must solve the heat equation.

blocks.title: Composite wall explorer
blocks.caption: Stack convection + conduction + conduction + convection resistances in series, watch the linear temperature drops and the single heat rate that flows through every layer.

blocks.text: Composite walls and the overall U

A wall with two fluids and several solid layers is just resistances in series:
$$R_{tot}=\frac{1}{h_1A}+\frac{L_A}{k_AA}+\frac{L_B}{k_BA}+\frac{1}{h_3A}$$
The heat rate is $q=(T_{\infty,1}-T_{\infty,3})/R_{tot}$. The **same** $q$ lets you back out every interface temperature by walking across one resistance at a time.

blocks.caption: Overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ ($\mathrm{W/m^2\,K}$). For a plane wall $U$ is just $1/(R_{tot}\cdot A)$.

blocks.title: Parallel resistances & contact resistance
blocks.text: If two materials sit side-by-side across the same $\Delta T$ (e.g. studs + insulation), they are in **parallel**: $1/R = 1/R_1 + 1/R_2$. A non-perfect joint between two solids adds a **thermal contact resistance** $R_{tc}=1/(h_cA)$ in series — it causes a temperature jump at the interface even though $q$ is continuous.

steps.label: Sketch the circuit
steps.text: Draw every resistance from fluid 1 to fluid 2 in order: convection, each conduction layer, convection.

steps.label: Sum
steps.text: Add series resistances; combine any parallel branch first. Get $R_{tot}$.

steps.label: Heat rate
steps.text: $q = (T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2})/R_{tot}$. This $q$ is the same everywhere.

steps.label: Walk across
steps.text: For any interface temperature, $q = \Delta T_{across\,one\,R} / R_{that\,one}$. Solve for the unknown $T$.

blocks.title: Worked: plane wall, find U, q and surface temperatures (Tutorial 6.7)
blocks.text:
**Given:** wall $s=0.15$ m, $k=1.37$ W/mK, fluids $T_{f1}=25^\circ$C ($h_i=10.5$), $T_{f2}=0^\circ$C ($h_o=52.3$). Find $q/A$, $T_{s1}$, $T_{s2}$.

**Per unit area** the resistances are $1/h_i=0.0952$, $s/k=0.1095$, $1/h_o=0.0191$ ($\mathrm{m^2K/W}$). Sum $=0.2238$.
$$\frac{q}{A}=\frac{25-0}{0.2238}=112\ \mathrm{W/m^2}$$
Surface 1: $T_{s1}=T_{f1}-\frac{q}{A}\cdot\frac{1}{h_i}=25-112(0.0952)=\mathbf{14.4^\circ C}$.
Surface 2: $T_{s2}=T_{s1}-\frac{q}{A}\cdot\frac{s}{k}=14.36-112(0.1095)=\mathbf{2.1^\circ C}$.

Note $q$ is the same through every layer; the temperature simply steps down across each resistance.
Series resistance reasoning

question.prompt: Three layers are in **series** between two fluids. Which quantity is the SAME across all three layers at steady state?

options.content: The temperature drop across each layer
The thermal resistance of each layer
The heat rate $q$ flowing through them
The thermal conductivity of each layer

question.explanation: Like current in a series electrical circuit, the **heat rate $q$ is identical** through every series resistance. A: temperature drop differs (larger across larger $R$). B: resistances generally differ. D: each material has its own $k$. That constant $q$ is exactly what lets you find interface temperatures one resistance at a time.
question.theory: Series thermal resistances carry the same heat rate; $\Delta T$ divides in proportion to each $R$ ($\Delta T_i = q\cdot R_i$).

blocks.text: Hollow cylinder (pipes)

For radial conduction the area grows with $r$, so the profile is **logarithmic**, not linear: $T(r)=C_1\ln r + C_2$. The conduction resistance of a cylindrical shell is $R=\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi L k}$. Convection at a curved surface uses the area at that radius, $A=2\pi r L$, so $R_{conv}=\dfrac{1}{2\pi r L\,h}$.

blocks.tex: q_r=\frac{T_{\infty1}-T_{\infty3}}{\dfrac{1}{2\pi r_1 L h_1}+\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi L k_A}+\dfrac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi L k_B}+\dfrac{1}{2\pi r_3 L h_3}}
blocks.caption: Composite hollow cylinder. Each convection resistance uses ITS OWN radius ($r_1$ inside, $r_3$ outside).

blocks.title: Per-metre vs total
blocks.text: Pipe answers are often given as $q/L$ (W per metre). If $L$ is not given, you can only find $q/L$ — divide every resistance term by dropping $L$, or equivalently write $q/L = 2\pi\,\Delta T / [\ln(r_2/r_1)/k + \dots]$. Don't mix W and W/m.
Critical radius of insulation

Counter-intuitively, adding insulation to a thin pipe or wire can **increase** heat loss. Insulation adds conduction resistance (good) but also increases the outer radius, which **enlarges the convective area** and lowers convection resistance (bad). For a cylinder these two effects compete.

blocks.caption: Critical radius. At $r_{ins} = r_c$ the total resistance is a MINIMUM, so heat loss is MAXIMUM.

blocks.title: What rc means practically
blocks.text:
- If $r_1 < r_c$: adding insulation up to $r_c$ first **increases** heat loss, then decreases it.
- If $r_1 > r_c$: any insulation immediately reduces heat loss (the normal case for big pipes).
- Example: with $k_{ins}=0.05$ and $h=10$, $r_c=5$ mm — relevant for thin wires, not for fat pipes.
Conduction WITH internal generation

When heat is generated inside (Joule heating, nuclear, chemical) at a volumetric rate $\dot q_g$ ($\mathrm{W/m^3}$), the profile becomes **parabolic** and $q$ is no longer constant. You cannot use a resistance circuit; integrate the heat equation with the boundary conditions instead.

blocks.caption: Generation profiles. Maximum $T$ is at the centre ($x=0$ or $r=0$) where the gradient is zero — no heat crosses the midplane/axis.

blocks.title: Joule heating gives qg
blocks.text: For a current-carrying conductor, $\dot q_g = \dfrac{P_e}{V}=\dfrac{\rho_e I^2/A_c}{A_c}=\dfrac{\rho_e I^2}{A_c^2}$ where $A_c=\pi R^2$ (or $\pi D^2/4$). Then surface energy balance $\dot q_g V = h S (T_s-T_\infty)$ gives the surface temperature, and the parabola gives the centre.

blocks.title: Worked: current-carrying wire centre temperature (Tutorial 6.8)
blocks.text:
**Given:** $D=8$ cm, $k=2.7$, $\rho_e=0.5\ \mu\Omega\mathrm{m}$, $I=2000$ A, $T_f=0^\circ$C, $h=20$.

Generation: $\dot q_g=\rho_e (I/A_c)^2$ with $A_c=\pi D^2/4=5.03\times10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m^2}$. $\dot q_g=0.5\times10^{-6}(2000/5.03\times10^{-3})^2=7.92\times10^4\ \mathrm{W/m^3}$.

Surface (energy balance $\dot q_g\cdot\pi D^2/4 = h\,\pi D\,(T_s-T_f)$): $T_s=\dfrac{\dot q_g D}{4h}=\dfrac{7.92\times10^4\cdot0.08}{4\cdot20}=\mathbf{79.2^\circ C}$.

Centre: $T_c=\dfrac{\dot q_g R^2}{4k}+T_s=\dfrac{7.92\times10^4(0.04)^2}{4(2.7)}+79.16=\mathbf{90.9^\circ C}$.
Fins (extended surfaces)

To boost convective heat loss $q=hA(T_s-T_\infty)$ when you cannot raise $h$ or $\Delta T$, you **add area** — fins. Heat conducts along the fin and convects off its sides. Define $\theta(x)=T(x)-T_\infty$. The fin equation $\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dx^2}-m^2\theta=0$ has the fin parameter $m$.

blocks.title: Fin temperature & efficiency
blocks.caption: Vary length, conductivity and $h$: see the exponential/cosh temperature decay along the fin and how efficiency drops as the fin gets long or $k$ gets small.
$P$ = perimeter, $A_c$ = cross-section area, $\theta_b$ = base excess temperature. For a very long fin $q_f = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\cdot\theta_b$.

blocks.text: **Effectiveness** $\varepsilon_f=\dfrac{q_f}{hA_c\theta_b}$ compares the finned heat rate to the bare base area — fins are worth it only if $\varepsilon_f>1$ (in practice $\gg1$). **Efficiency** $\eta_f=\dfrac{q_f}{hA_f\theta_b}$ compares the actual heat rate to an ideal fin held entirely at $T_b$ ($A_f$ = total fin surface). $\eta_f\le1$ always.

blocks.title: Don't confuse the two ratios
blocks.text: Effectiveness divides by the **small base area** $A_c$ (so it can be ~50). Efficiency divides by the **whole fin surface** $A_f$ (so it is between 0 and 1). Mixing the denominators is a classic exam error.

blocks.title: Worked: fin array (Tutorial 6.5)
blocks.text:
**Given:** $w=1$ m, $L=40$ mm, $s=1.5$ mm, $k=200$, $h=23$, $T_0=90^\circ$C, $T_f=40^\circ$C, $n=74$ fins on base $b=1$ m.

$\theta_b=50$ K. $P=2(s+w)=2.003$ m, $A_c=sw=1.5\times10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m^2}$. $m=\sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}=12.39\ \mathrm{m^{-1}}$.

One fin: $q_f=kA_c m\theta_b\tanh(mL)=85.2$ W. Array: $q_{f,tot}=n q_f=6308$ W.
Bare base between fins: $A_{free}=bw-nws=0.889\ \mathrm{m^2}$, $q_{bare}=hA_{free}\theta_b=1022$ W.
Total $q=7330$ W.
$\varepsilon_f=q_f/(hA_c\theta_b)=85.2/(23\cdot1.5\times10^{-3}\cdot50)=\mathbf{49.4}$ (fins very worthwhile).
$\eta_f=q_f/(hS_f\theta_b)$ with $S_f=2Lw+2Ls=0.08\ \mathrm{m^2}$ gives $\mathbf{0.93}$.
Fin worthwhile?

question.prompt: The use of fins is **not** justified when the fin effectiveness $\varepsilon_f$ is:

options.content: independent of the fin's thermal conductivity

question.explanation: $\varepsilon_f$ is the finned heat rate divided by what the bare base would lose. If $\varepsilon_f<1$ the fin actually **reduces** heat transfer (it adds conduction resistance), so it is not justified. A: $\varepsilon_f\gg1$ is exactly when fins ARE justified. C: effectiveness does depend on $k$ (high-$k$ fins perform better). D: wrong since (B) is correct.
question.theory: Fins add area but also a conduction resistance; they help only if effectiveness exceeds 1, ideally far above 1.

blocks.text: Transient conduction: lumped capacitance

When a body is suddenly dunked in a fluid, its temperature changes in time. If the body conducts heat internally **much** faster than it loses it by convection, the whole body stays nearly uniform and $T=T(t)$ only. This is the **lumped-capacitance** method. Energy balance: $-hA_s(T-T_\infty)=\rho c V \dfrac{dT}{dt}$.

blocks.tex: \frac{\theta}{\theta_i}=\frac{T-T_\infty}{T_i-T_\infty}=\exp\!\Big(-\frac{hA_s}{\rho c V}\,t\Big)=e^{-t/t_0},\qquad t_0=\frac{\rho c V}{hA_s}=R_t C_t
blocks.caption: Exponential cooling. Time constant $t_0$: after $t = t_0$ the excess temperature has fallen to 0.368 of its start.
Total energy released up to time $t$, and the instantaneous heat rate at time $t$.

blocks.text: $Bi=\dfrac{hL^*}{k}$ with characteristic length $L^*=\dfrac{V}{A_s}$. It is the ratio of internal conduction resistance to surface convection resistance. **Lumped capacitance is valid only if $Bi<0.1$** — then internal gradients are negligible. $Bi$ is dimensionless. For a sphere $L^*=R/3=D/6$; for a slab cooled both sides $L^*=L/2$ (half-thickness), but using $V/A_s$ always works.

blocks.title: Use kelvin differences, but °C ratios are fine
blocks.text: The ratio $\theta/\theta_i$ uses temperature **differences**, so °C and K give the same number — no conversion needed there. But the exponent argument must be consistent in seconds. And remember $L^*=V/A_s$: a sphere gives $D/6$, NOT $D/2$. Using the radius or diameter directly is the favourite mistake.

steps.label: Compute Bi
steps.text: $L^* = V/A_s$, then $Bi = hL^*/k$. Confirm $Bi < 0.1$ before using lumped capacitance.

steps.label: Form theta
steps.text: $\theta_i = T_i - T_\infty$, $\theta = T(t) - T_\infty$ (differences, K or °C).

steps.label: Time to reach T
steps.text: $t = -(\rho c V)/(h A_s) \cdot \ln(\theta/\theta_i)$, or use $m\cdot c$ for $V\cdot\rho$.

blocks.title: Worked: quenching a steel sphere — Biot number (Quiz 14)
blocks.text:
**Given:** $D=0.18$ m, $\rho=8000$, $k=50$, $h=20$.

$L^*=\dfrac{V}{A_s}=\dfrac{R}{3}=\dfrac{0.09}{3}=0.03$ m.
$$Bi=\frac{hL^*}{k}=\frac{20\times0.03}{50}=\mathbf{0.012}<0.1$$
So lumped capacitance is valid. The distractors come from using $L^*=R=0.09$ ($Bi=0.036$), $L^*=D=0.18$ ($Bi=0.072$), or $D/3$ ($Bi=0.024$).
Biot number meaning

options.content: inversely proportional to the characteristic dimension $L^*$

question.explanation: $Bi=hL^*/k$ has units $(\mathrm{W/m^2K})(\mathrm{m})/(\mathrm{W/mK})=$ dimensionless. A: it is inversely proportional to $k$ ($k$ in the denominator). B: it is proportional to $L^*$ (numerator). D: it does not depend on temperature at all.
question.theory: $Bi = hL^*/k$ is a dimensionless ratio of internal conduction resistance to surface convection resistance; $Bi < 0.1$ validates lumped capacitance.

practice.prompt: Fourier's law states that the conduction heat rate is:

options.content: inversely proportional to the thermal conductivity
proportional to the heat transfer surface area
inversely proportional to the temperature gradient

practice.explanation: $q=-kA\,dT/dx$: the rate is proportional to area $A$, to $k$, and to the gradient. B is correct. A and D misstate the role of $k$ (rate rises with $k$, it is not independent of nor inversely related to it). C is wrong because a larger gradient gives MORE heat, not less.
practice.theory: Fourier: $q$ is proportional to $A$, $k$ and the temperature gradient; the minus sign sets the direction (hot to cold).

practice.prompt: The units of the overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ are:
practice.explanation: $q=UA\Delta T$, so $U=q/(A\Delta T)$ has units $\mathrm{W}/(\mathrm{m^2K})$. A is the units of a unit-area resistance ($1/U$). B is the units of a total resistance $R$. D is a heat flux, missing the per-kelvin.
practice.theory: $U$ appears in $q = U\cdot A\cdot\Delta T$; it is $\mathrm{W/m^2K}$, the reciprocal of the area-based total resistance.

practice.prompt: A flat wall (no generation), thickness $25$ cm, area $10\,\mathrm{m^2}$, $k=0.5$ W/mK, is between fluids at $20^\circ$C ($h=8$) and $-20^\circ$C ($h=25$). The total thermal resistance between the two fluids is:
practice.explanation: $R_{tot}=\frac{1}{A}(\frac1{h_1}+\frac{s}{k}+\frac1{h_2})=\frac1{10}(0.125+0.5+0.04)=0.0665\approx0.067$ K/W. A drops the $1/A$ factor (uses the per-area sum 0.665, then mis-scales). C is ten times too big (forgot to divide by $A$). D has the right number but inverted units (resistance is K/W, not W/K).

practice.prompt: A stainless-steel sphere, $D=18$ cm, $\rho=8000$, $k=50$, is quenched with $h=20\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$. The Biot number is:
practice.explanation: $L^*=V/A_s=R/3=0.03$ m, so $Bi=hL^*/k=20\cdot0.03/50=0.012$. C uses $L^*=R=0.09$ (forgot the $/3$). B uses $L^*=D=0.18$. A uses $L^*\approx D/2.4$. Only D uses the correct $V/A_s=D/6$.
practice.theory: For a sphere $L^* = V/A_s = R/3 = D/6$; $Bi = hL^*/k$.

practice.prompt: The use of fins is NOT justified when the fin effectiveness $\varepsilon_f$ is:

options.content: independent of the fin's thermal conductivity
exactly equal to the fin efficiency

practice.explanation: $\varepsilon_f=q_f/(hA_c\theta_b)$ compares finned to bare-base heat rate; $\varepsilon_f<1$ means the fin reduces heat transfer, so it is not justified. C is the opposite (when fins are clearly worthwhile). A is false — effectiveness rises with $k$. D mixes two different ratios with different denominators.
practice.theory: Fins help only when effectiveness $> 1$ (ideally $\gg1$); below 1 the added conduction resistance hurts.

practice.prompt: Adding insulation to a thin cylindrical wire can INCREASE its heat loss. The outer radius at which heat loss is maximum is:
practice.explanation: Setting $dR_{tot}/dr_{ins}=0$ for a cylinder gives $r_c=k_{ins}/h$, where $R_{tot}$ is minimum so $q$ is maximum. A inverts the ratio. B multiplies instead of dividing. D introduces a spurious factor of 2 (that factor appears for a sphere, not a cylinder).
practice.theory: Critical radius of a cylinder: $r_c = k_{ins}/h$. Below $r_c$ insulation raises heat loss; above $r_c$ it lowers it.

practice.prompt: A composite pipe carries the resistances $R_{conv,in}, R_{cond,A}, R_{cond,B}, R_{conv,out}$ in series with $T_{\infty1}=250^\circ$C, $T_{\infty2}=25^\circ$C and $R_{tot}=0.0276$ K/W. The heat rate is closest to:
practice.explanation: $q=(T_{\infty1}-T_{\infty2})/R_{tot}=225/0.0276\approx8152$ W. C forgets to divide by $R_{tot}$ (just the $\Delta T$ in °C). D divides $\Delta T$ by $R_{tot}$ inverted / uses wrong powers. B uses a wrong $\Delta T$ (e.g. an interface temperature).
practice.theory: $q = \Delta T_{overall} / R_{tot}$ for any series resistance network (plane or cylindrical).

practice.prompt: In steady 1-D conduction through a single plane wall with no generation, the temperature profile is:

options.content: parabolic, with a maximum inside the wall

practice.explanation: $d^2T/dx^2=0$ integrates to $T=C_1x+C_2$, a straight line, so $q$ is constant. A is the profile WITH generation. B is the cylindrical (radial) profile. D is the fin temperature profile, not a plane wall.
practice.theory: Plane wall, no generation: linear $T(x)$, constant $q$. Generation makes it parabolic; cylinder makes it logarithmic.

practice.prompt: A current-carrying wire generates heat uniformly. At steady state the maximum temperature occurs:

options.content: at the surface, where convection removes heat
on the axis ($r=0$), where the gradient is zero
it is uniform, so there is no maximum

practice.explanation: $T(r)=\frac{\dot q_g}{4k}(R^2-r^2)+T_s$ peaks at $r=0$, where $dT/dr=0$ (by symmetry no heat crosses the axis). A is the coldest point (heat leaves there). C has no special meaning. D is false — with generation there IS an internal gradient.
practice.theory: With uniform generation, the centre is hottest because all generated heat must flow outward; the gradient is zero on the symmetry axis.

practice.prompt: A body is quenched in a fluid. The lumped-capacitance model gives $\theta/\theta_i=e^{-t/t_0}$. After one time constant $t=t_0$, the excess temperature $\theta=T-T_\infty$ has dropped to about:
practice.explanation: $e^{-1}=0.368$, so $\theta=0.368\,\theta_i$ at $t=t_0$. A ($0.632=1-e^{-1}$) is the FRACTION already lost, not what remains. B would need $t=t_0\ln2$. D ($e^{-2}=0.135$) is after two time constants.
practice.theory: Exponential decay: at $t = t_0$ the remaining excess temperature is $e^{-1} \approx 0.368$ of the initial.

practice.prompt: For a long single fin with an adiabatic tip, $m=\sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}$. If the fin's thermal conductivity $k$ is increased (all else fixed), the heat rate $q_f=\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\tanh(mL)$:

options.content: increases, because the $\sqrt{k}$ factor grows faster than $\tanh(mL)$ shrinks

practice.explanation: Higher $k$ raises the $\sqrt{kA_c}$ prefactor and keeps the fin nearer $T_b$ (better temperature distribution), so $q_f$ rises even though $m\propto1/\sqrt{k}$ falls slightly and $\tanh(mL)$ drops a little; the prefactor dominates. A wrongly assumes $m$ controls $q_f$. C is false — $k$ does not cancel. D misreads $\tanh$, which is never zero for $L>0$.
practice.theory: $q_f$ scales with $\sqrt{k}$; a more conductive fin spreads heat better and transfers more, the standard reason for using aluminium/copper fins.

practice.prompt: Two solid layers are placed SIDE-BY-SIDE across the same overall temperature difference (a parallel arrangement). The equivalent resistance is:
practice.explanation: Parallel paths share the same $\Delta T$ but carry separate heat rates that add, giving $1/R_{eq}=1/R_1+1/R_2$ (like parallel electrical resistors). A is the SERIES rule. B has wrong units (product of two resistances). D is only the average, valid for nothing general.
practice.theory: Same as electrical circuits: series resistances add; parallel resistances combine by reciprocals.

practice.prompt: A plane wall ($s_1=10$ cm, $k_1=75$, $A=10\,\mathrm{m^2}$) sits between fluids at $300^\circ$C ($h_1=174.4$) and $25^\circ$C ($h_2=10.5$). The overall coefficient $U$ is closest to:
practice.explanation: $1/U=1/h_1+s_1/k_1+1/h_2=0.00573+0.00133+0.0952=0.1024$, so $U=9.77\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$. A is $1/U$ (forgot to invert). C is ten times too big. D is the heat rate in kW (26.9 kW), not $U$.
practice.theory: $U = 1/(1/h_1 + s/k + 1/h_2)$ for a plane wall; the dominant resistance here is the low-$h$ side.

practice.prompt: The thermal-resistance (electrical) analogy can be used:

options.content: always, including with internal heat generation
only at steady state and without volumetric generation
only for cylinders, not plane walls

practice.explanation: The lecture states the analogy is valid only at steady state and with no generation — generation makes $q$ vary along the path, so a single resistance cannot represent it. A directly contradicts this. B is wrong (it is for steady state). D is false — it applies to both geometries.
practice.theory: Resistance networks need a constant $q$ along the path: valid only steady-state, no generation.

practice.prompt: A sphere cools by lumped capacitance with $\theta_i=475$ K (so $T_i-T_\infty$). After a time you measure $\theta=75$ K. The time is $\tau=-\frac{\rho V c}{hA_s}\ln(\theta/\theta_i)$. If $\theta/\theta_i$ were mistakenly inverted, the computed time would be:
practice.explanation: Correctly $\ln(75/475)<0$, and the leading minus sign makes $\tau>0$. Inverting to $\ln(475/75)>0$ gives $\tau<0$, a negative (unphysical) time — a useful sanity check. A is false; $\ln(1/x)=-\ln x$, not equal. C and D are not what sign inversion produces.
practice.theory: In cooling, $\theta/\theta_i < 1$ so its log is negative; the minus sign restores a positive time. A negative time signals you flipped the ratio.

exam.title: Composite plane wall: U, heat rate and surface temperatures
exam.statement: A plane wall (steady, 1-D, no generation) has thickness $s_1=10$ cm, conductivity $k_1=75$ W/mK and area $A=10\,\mathrm{m^2}$. It separates a hot fluid at $T_{f1}=300^\circ$C ($h_1=174.4\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$) from a cold fluid at $T_{f2}=25^\circ$C ($h_2=10.5\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$). Find the overall coefficient $U$, the heat rate $q$, and the two wall-surface temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$.

steps.title: Overall U
steps.content: $U = 1/(R_{tot}\cdot A) = 1/(1.024\times10^{-2}\cdot10) = 9.77\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$. The outside convection (low $h$) dominates the resistance.

steps.title: Heat rate
steps.content: $q = (T_{f1} - T_{f2})/R_{tot} = 275/1.024\times10^{-2} = 26.87$ kW. (Same as $q = U\cdot A\cdot\Delta T = 9.77\cdot10\cdot275$.)

steps.title: Surface temperatures
steps.content: $T_1 = T_{f1} - q\cdot R_1 = 300 - 26870\cdot5.73\times10^{-4} = 284.6$ °C. $T_2 = T_{f2} + q\cdot R_3 = 25 + 26870\cdot9.52\times10^{-3} = 281$ °C. The small drop across the metal ($T_1-T_2 \approx 3.6$ °C) reflects its tiny conduction resistance.

exam.tips: Walk across one resistance at a time using the SAME $q$. Keep °C for differences (fine) but be consistent. The lowest-$h$ side carries most of the temperature drop.

exam.title: Insulated pipe: heat loss and interface temperatures
exam.statement: A steel pipe ($d_1=10$ cm inside, $d_2=15$ cm outside, $k_1=40$ W/mK), length $L=10$ m, is covered with $s=2$ cm of insulation ($k_2=0.5$ W/mK), so $d_3=19$ cm. Inside: $T_{f1}=250^\circ$C, $h_1=100$; outside: $T_{f2}=25^\circ$C, $h_2=10$. Find the heat rate $q$ and the temperatures $T_1$ (inner wall), $T_3$ (outer insulation surface).

steps.title: Inner-wall temperature
steps.content: $q = 2\pi r_1 L h_1 (T_{f1} - T_1) \Rightarrow T_1 = T_{f1} - q\cdot R_{conv,in} = 250 - 8152\cdot3.18\times10^{-3} = 224$ °C.

steps.title: Outer-surface temperature
steps.content: $q = 2\pi r_3 L h_2 (T_3 - T_{f2}) \Rightarrow T_3 = T_{f2} + q\cdot R_{conv,out} = 25 + 8152\cdot1.68\times10^{-2} = 161.3$ °C.

exam.tips: Each convection resistance uses ITS OWN radius ($r_1$ inside, $r_3$ outside). If $L$ were not given, report $q/L = 2\pi\cdot225/[\ln(r_2/r_1)/k_1 + \ln(r_3/r_2)/k_2 + \dots]$.

exam.title: Fin array on a heat sink
exam.statement: A heat sink base $b=1$ m by $w=1$ m at $T_0=90^\circ$C carries $n=74$ rectangular fins, each length $L=40$ mm, thickness $s=1.5$ mm, width $w=1$ m, $k=200$ W/mK. Ambient $T_f=40^\circ$C, $h=23\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$. Assume an adiabatic tip. Find the single-fin heat rate $q_{fin}$, the total heat rate from the sink $q_{tot}$, the fin effectiveness $\varepsilon_f$ and efficiency $\eta_f$.

steps.title: Single-fin heat rate
steps.content: $q_{fin} = k A_c m \theta_b \cdot \tanh(mL) = 200\cdot1.5\times10^{-3}\cdot12.39\cdot50\cdot\tanh(0.4956) = 185.9\cdot0.4585 = 85.2$ W.

steps.title: Array + bare base
steps.content: $q_{fin,tot} = n\cdot q_{fin} = 74\cdot85.2 = 6308$ W. Free base area $A_{free} = b\cdot w - n\cdot w\cdot s = 1 - 74\cdot1\cdot1.5\times10^{-3} = 0.889\ \mathrm{m^2}$. $q_{bare} = h\cdot A_{free}\cdot\theta_b = 23\cdot0.889\cdot50 = 1022$ W. $q_{tot} = 6308 + 1022 = 7330$ W.

steps.title: Effectiveness
steps.content: $\varepsilon_f = q_{fin}/(h A_c \theta_b) = 85.2/(23\cdot1.5\times10^{-3}\cdot50) = 85.2/1.725 = 49.4$. Far above 1, so the fins are very worthwhile.

exam.tips: Effectiveness divides by the small base area $A_c$ (gives ~50); efficiency divides by the whole fin surface $S_{fin}$ (gives $<1$). Don't forget the bare base between fins contributes to $q_{tot}$.

exam.title: Transient quenching: time, energy, instantaneous rate
exam.statement: A metal block, $m=8$ kg, $L=10$ cm (cube), $k=200$ W/mK, $c=500$ J/kgK, starts at $T_1=120^\circ$C and is quenched in a fluid at $T_f=20^\circ$C with $h=20\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$. (A) Verify lumped capacitance applies and find the time $\tau$ to reach $T_2=40^\circ$C. (B) After $\tau'=30$ min find the energy released $Q$ and the instantaneous heat rate.

steps.title: Biot check
steps.content: $L^* = V/A_s = L^3/(6L^2) = L/6 = 0.0167$ m. $Bi = hL^*/k = 20\cdot0.0167/200 = 1.67\times10^{-3} \ll 0.1$. Lumped capacitance is valid.

steps.title: Time to reach 40 °C
steps.content: $\theta_1 = T_1-T_f = 100$ K, $\theta_2 = T_2-T_f = 20$ K. $\tau = -(m c)/(h A_s)\cdot\ln(\theta_2/\theta_1) = -(8\cdot500)/(20\cdot0.06)\cdot\ln(0.2) = -3333\cdot(-1.609) = 5365$ s $\approx 89$ min.

steps.title: Temperature at τ' = 1800 s
steps.content: $\theta^* = \theta_1\cdot\exp(-hA_s \tau'/(mc)) = 100\cdot\exp(-20\cdot0.06\cdot1800/(8\cdot500)) = 100\cdot\exp(-0.54) = 58.3$ K, so $T = 78.3$ °C.

steps.title: Instantaneous heat rate
steps.content: $q_{inst} = h A_s \theta^* = 20\cdot0.06\cdot58.3 = 70$ W.

steps.title: Energy released in 0→1800 s
steps.content: $Q = m c (\theta_1 - \theta^*) = 8\cdot500\cdot(100 - 58.3) = 167$ kJ. (Equivalently $Q = mc\cdot\theta_1\cdot(1-e^{-t/t_0})$.)

exam.finalAnswer: $Bi = 1.7\times10^{-3}$ (valid); $\tau \approx 5365$ s $\approx 89$ min; at 30 min: $q_{inst} \approx 70$ W, $Q \approx 167$ kJ.
exam.tips: Use $mc$ in place of $\rho V c$ when mass is given. Temperature DIFFERENCES (K or °C) are interchangeable in the ratio. Always check $Bi < 0.1$ before trusting the lumped model.

exam.title: Conceptual: derive the critical radius and explain the curve
exam.statement: For a cylinder of radius $r_1$ at fixed surface temperature, insulation (conductivity $k_{ins}$) is added to outer radius $r_{ins}$, with outside convection coefficient $h$. (a) Write the total resistance per unit length. (b) Derive the critical radius $r_c$. (c) Sketch and explain how heat loss varies with $r_{ins}$ and state when adding insulation actually reduces heat loss.
exam.given: Cylinder $r_1$, insulation $k_{ins}$ to $r_{ins}$, outside $h$, ambient $T_\infty$; steady, no generation

steps.title: Total resistance
steps.content: Per unit length, $R_{tot} = \ln(r_{ins}/r_1)/(2\pi k_{ins}) + 1/(2\pi r_{ins} h)$. The first term (conduction) grows with $r_{ins}$; the second (convection) SHRINKS because the area grows.

steps.title: Minimise R_tot
steps.content: $dR_{tot}/dr_{ins} = 1/(2\pi k_{ins} r_{ins}) - 1/(2\pi r_{ins}^2 h) = 0 \Rightarrow r_{ins} = k_{ins}/h$.

steps.title: Confirm it's a minimum
steps.content: The second derivative is positive at $r_{ins} = k_{ins}/h$, so $R_{tot}$ is a MINIMUM there — meaning $q = \Delta T/R_{tot}$ is a MAXIMUM. Hence $r_c = k_{ins}/h$.

steps.title: The curve
steps.content: Plot $q$ vs $r_{ins}$: $q$ rises from $r_1$ to a peak at $r_c$, then falls. If $r_1 < r_c$, the first bit of insulation INCREASES heat loss (bad for thermal protection, good for cooling a wire). If $r_1 > r_c$, any insulation immediately reduces heat loss.

steps.title: Practical statement
steps.content: Insulation reliably reduces heat loss only when the bare radius already exceeds the critical radius, or when you insulate well past $r_c$. For a wire with $k_{ins} = 0.05$, $h = 10$, $r_c = 5$ mm — thin wires can be cooled MORE by a thin sheath.

exam.finalAnswer: $R_{tot} = \ln(r_{ins}/r_1)/(2\pi k_{ins}) + 1/(2\pi r_{ins} h)$; $r_c = k_{ins}/h$ ($R_{tot}$ minimum, $q$ maximum); insulation lowers heat loss only once $r_{ins} > r_c$ (or if $r_1$ already $> r_c$).
exam.tips: The competition is conduction resistance (up) vs convection area (up $\Rightarrow$ convection resistance down). This effect exists for cylinders/spheres, NOT for plane walls where added thickness always helps.
